sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 1

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 1:1
items.html:
First of all, we must know that where we deal with spiritual matters, abstracted from time, space, and motion — and all the more so with Godliness — we have no words by which to contemplate and express ourselves, for our entire vocabulary is taken from the sensations of the imaginary senses. How can words assist us where sense and imagination do not govern? For example, even if you take the subtlest of words, such as "Lights," it too is imaginary, borrowed from the light of the sun, or from a felt light of contentment and the like. If so, how can matters of Godliness be expressed by them? They would certainly offer the examiner nothing true. This is all the more so where these words must reveal, in writing in a book, the give-and-take of the wisdom, as is customary in the research of any wisdom: if we fail with even one word that does not suit its purpose, the examiner is instantly confused and will not find his hands and feet in the entire matter. Therefore, the sages of Kabbalah chose for themselves a special language, which we may call "the language of the branches," since there is no essence, nor conduct of an essence, in this world that does not extend from its root in the upper world. On the contrary, the beginning of every being in this world starts in the upper world and afterwards cascades down to this world. Hence, the sages found a language ready-made, without labor, by which to convey their attainments to one another, by word of mouth and in writing, from generation to generation: they took the names of the branches in this world, each name explaining itself, as though pointing a finger at its upper root in the system of the upper worlds.
This will settle your mind about the perplexing expressions you often find in books of Kabbalah, some of them foreign to the human spirit. Once they had chosen this language — the language of the branches — how could they omit any branch along the way, declining to use it because of its lowly degree, and fail to express the desired concept by it, when there is no other branch in our world to take in its place? For just as two hairs do not feed from the same follicle, we have no two branches relating to a single root. Nor is it possible to lose the item of the wisdom that is bound to that lowly expression — such a loss would corrupt and confuse the entire expanse of the wisdom, for there is no other wisdom among all the wisdoms of the world in which matters are so interlinked, by way of cause and effect, cause and consequence, as the wisdom of Kabbalah, where matters are bound and tied to one another from head to toe like one long chain. Therefore, there is no freedom of will here to exchange and replace these appellations, bad for good; we are obliged always to bring precisely the branch that points at its upper root, and to elaborate upon it until an exact definition is established for the eyes of the discerning examiner.
Indeed, those whose eyes have not yet been opened to the sights of Heaven, who have not yet acquired the proficiency in the relations between the branches of this world and their roots in the upper worlds, stand here like the blind groping at a wall: they will not understand even one word in its true meaning, for every word is the name of some branch in relation to its root — unless they receive the interpretation from the mouth of an accomplished sage who makes himself available to explain the matter in the spoken language, which is necessarily like translating from one language to another — from the language of the branches into the spoken language — for only then can the spiritual concept be explained as it is.
This is what I have labored to do in this commentary: to explain the ten Sefirot as the Godly sage, the ARI, instructed us — in their spiritual purity, abstracted from any tangible concepts — so that any beginner may approach the wisdom without failing in any materialization or error. With the understanding of these ten Sefirot, a door will also open to examine and to know how to understand the rest of the matters of this wisdom.

Chapter One
"Know that before the emanated beings were emanated and the created beings created, an upper, simple Light had filled the whole of reality," etc. (Etz Chaim, Gate 1, Palace 1). These words require explanation: before the worlds were emanated, how was there a place of reality that the simple Light filled entirely? Likewise, the ascent of the will to restrict Himself in order to bring the perfection of His deeds to light — the book seems to imply that some deficiency, God forbid, was already there; and likewise the matter of the middle point, "in Him, precisely," where the restriction occurred — most perplexing, since it has already been said that there is no Rosh [head] and no Sof [end] there, so how is there a middle? Indeed, these matters are deeper than the sea, and I am therefore compelled to elaborate in their explanation.

There is nothing in the whole of reality that is not included in Ein Sof; the concepts that are opposites to us are included in Him in the secret of "One, Unique, and Unified"
Know that there is no essence of any being in the world — whether tangible to our senses or conceived by the eye of our mind — that is not included in the Creator, for all of them come to us from Him; and is there one who gives what he does not possess? This matter is already well explained in the books. Yet we must understand: concepts that are separate for us, or opposite — for example, the concept of wisdom differs from the concept of sweetness, wisdom and sweetness being two separate concepts; likewise, the concept of the operator certainly differs from the concept of the operation, operator and operation necessarily being two distinct concepts; and all the more so opposite concepts, such as sweetness and bitterness, each certainly discerned on its own — yet with Him, wisdom and pleasure, sweetness and pungency, operation and operator, and all such differing and opposite forms are included as one in His simple Light, without any distinction or difference among them at all, in one single concept: "One, Unique, and Unified." "One" indicates that He is in a single equivalence. "Unique" indicates that all that extends from Him — all those multiplicities — are with Him in the form of unique, like His own Essence. "Unified" indicates that although He performs a multiplicity of operations, one force performs them all, and they all return and unite in the form of unique; this unique form swallows up all the forms that appear in His operations. This is a very subtle matter, and not every mind can bear it. Thus the Ramban interpreted for us His oneness in the secret of "One, Unique, and Unified," in his commentary on Sefer Yetzira (Chapter 1, Mishnah 7): there is a difference between One, Unique, and Unified — the Explicated Name, when He unites to act with one force, is called Unified; when He divides to perform His operation, each part of Him is called Unique; and when He is in a single equivalence, He is called One. Thus far his pure words. Meaning: "unites to act with one force" — He acts to bestow goodness, as befits His unity, and there is no change in His operations. "When He divides to perform His operation" — when His operations differ from one another and He appears, God forbid, as one who does good and one who does harm — then He is called Unique, for all His differing operations have a single outcome: to do good. He is thus Unique in each and every operation, unchanged by His differing operations. And when He is in a single equivalence, He is called One: "One" points at His Essence, in which all the opposites stand in a single equivalence — as written above, and as Maimonides wrote, that with Him the knower, the known, and the knowledge are one, for His thoughts are far higher than our thoughts, and His ways than our ways.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 1:2
items.html:
Two discernments in bestowal: before it comes into reception, and after it has come into reception
Go and learn from the eaters of the manna. The manna is called "bread from Heaven" because it did not materialize when clothing itself in this world. Our sages said that each and every one tasted in it whatever he wished. It follows that it necessarily contained opposite forms: one tasted it sweet, another tasted it pungent and bitter; the manna itself was necessarily comprised of both opposites together — for is there one who gives what he does not possess? And how can there be two opposites in a single carrier? It must be that the manna is simple, abstracted from both flavors, yet comprised of them in such a way that the corporeal receiver distinguishes for himself the flavor he wants. Understand every spiritual thing in this way: in itself it is unique and simple, yet it comprises the entire multiplicity of forms in the world; and when it comes into the hands of a corporeal, limited receiver, the receiver makes of it one distinct form out of the whole multiplicity of forms united in that spiritual essence. Accordingly, two discernments should always be made in His bestowal: the first is the form of the essence of the upper abundance before it comes into reception, while it is still simple, all-inclusive Light; the second is after the abundance has come into reception, whereby it acquires one distinct, partial form, according to the quality of the receiver.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 1:3
items.html:
How can it be understood that the soul is a part of Godliness
By this we come to understand what the Kabbalists said about the essence of the soul, and these are their words: the soul is literally "a part of God above," and there is no change whatsoever in her from the "Whole," except that the soul is a part and not the "Whole." It is like a stone hewn from a mountain: the essence of the mountain and the essence of the stone are the same, and there is no distinction between the stone and the mountain except that the stone is only a part of the mountain while the mountain is the "Whole." Thus far the essence of their words. On the face of it, these words are utterly perplexing; and hardest of all is to understand how it is possible to speak of separation and of a part in Godliness — even to the point of likening it to a stone hewn from a mountain. A stone is hewn from a mountain by an axe and a hammer; but in Godliness, as it were — how, and by what, would they be parted from one another?

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 1:4
items.html:
The spiritual is divided by the force of disparity of form, as the corporeal is divided by an axe
Before we come to the explanation, let us clarify the essence of separation as it applies in spirituality. Know that spiritual entities part from one another only by disparity of form: if one spiritual thing acquires two forms, it is no longer one, but two. Let me explain this through the souls of people, which are spiritual as well. The spiritual law is known — its form is simple, as said above; yet there is certainly a multiplicity of souls, as many as the bodies in which the souls shine — but they are distinguished from one another by the disparity of form in each and every one. As our sages said: just as their faces are not similar to one another, so their views are not similar to one another. And the body has this quality, that through it the forms of the souls are distinguished, until each soul can be discerned on its own — this one a good soul, that one a bad soul, and so on with the distinct forms.
You see, then, that just as a corporeal thing is divided, cut, and separated by an axe, and by a motion that puts distance between one part and another, so a spiritual thing is divided, cut, and separated by reason of the disparity of form between one part and another; and according to the measure of the disparity, so is the distance between one part and another measured. Remember this well.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 1:5
items.html:
How can disparity of form be conceived in the creation, with respect to Ein Sof
However, we have only settled the matter for this world, in the souls of people. But regarding the soul, of which they said that she is "a part of God above," it is not yet clarified how she is distinguished from Godliness so that she may be called a part of God. We cannot say it is by disparity of form, God forbid, for we have already clarified that Godliness is simple Light, comprising the entire multiplicity of forms and oppositions of form in the world within His simple oneness, in the secret of One, Unique, and Unified, as above. If so, how shall we conceive a disparity of form in the soul, by which she would differ from Godliness and thereby be parted, acquiring the name of a "part" of Him?
In truth, this question applies with still greater force to the Light of Ein Sof prior to the restriction. For this reality before us — all the worlds, upper and lower together — is examined in two discernments. The first is the form of this entire reality as it was before the restriction, when everything was without boundary and without end; this discernment is called "the Light of Ein Sof." The second is the form of this entire reality from the restriction downward, where everything is in boundary and measure; this discernment is called the four worlds — Atzilut, Beria, Yetzira, and Assiya. It is known that of His Essence, "no thought can perceive Him at all"; He has no name and no appellation, for whatever we do not attain, how shall we define it by a name? — a name means attainment, indicating that we have attained it in the discernment of that name. Certainly, then, in His Essence there is no name or appellation whatsoever; all the names and appellations exist only in His Light, which expands from Him. That expansion of His Light prior to the restriction, which filled the whole of reality without boundary and without end, is called Ein Sof [no end]. Accordingly, we must understand by what the Light of Ein Sof is defined in itself, departing, so to speak, from His Essence, so that we can define it by a name — the very question we asked above regarding the soul.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 1:6
items.html:
Explaining what our sages said, that labor and toil were instituted for the reward of the souls, for "one who eats of that which is not his own fears to look upon his own face"
To understand even a little in this lofty place, we must elaborate. Let us inquire into the pivot of this entire reality before us, and its general purpose — for is there an operator without a purpose? What, then, is this purpose, for whose sake He brought forth this whole reality before us, in the upper worlds and in the lower? Our sages have already instructed us in many places that all the worlds were created only for Israel who keep Torah and Mitzvot, etc., and this is well known. Yet we must understand the question our sages asked about it: if the intention in the creation of the worlds is to delight His creatures, why did He create this corporeal, turbid world, so full of torments? Without it, He could certainly delight the souls as much as He wished, as it were; why bring the soul into a body so foul and filthy? And they answered: "one who eats of that which is not his own fears to look upon his own face" — meaning that in every free gift there lies the flaw of shame; and to spare the souls this flaw, He created this world, in which there is a reality of work, so that in the future they would enjoy the fruit of their labor, taking their full reward in return for their toil, and thereby be spared the flaw of shame.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 1:7
items.html:
What relation is there between seventy years of work and eternal delight — and there is no greater free gift than this
These words of theirs are exceedingly difficult on every side. First: our chief aim and prayer is "grant us from the treasure of a free gift," and our sages said that the treasure of a free gift is reserved only for the greatest souls in the world. Harder still is the heart of their answer — that a free gift carries a great deficiency, namely the shame that meets every receiver of a free gift, and that to remedy this He prepared this world, in which there is a reality of toil and labor, so that in the world to come they would take their reward in return for their trouble and the toil of their hands. Their answer is most perplexing. What is this like? Like a man who says to his friend: work with me one small moment, and in return I shall give you every pleasure and every treasure in the world, all the days of your life. There is no greater free gift than that, for the reward bears no equivalence whatsoever to the work: the work is in this world, a passing world that is worth nothing next to the reward and the pleasure of the eternal world — what value has the quantity of a passing world against the quantity of an eternal world? All the more so the quality of the toil, which is as nothing against the quality of the reward, as our sages said: "the Creator will grant every righteous one 310 worlds," etc. Nor can one say that He gives a portion of the reward in return for their toil and the rest as a free gift — for then, what have the sages gained by their answer? The flaw of shame remains in the rest of the gift! Rather, their words are not to be understood literally; there is a deep intention here.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 1:8
items.html:
In one thought the whole of reality was emanated and created; it is the operator, it is the very operation, it is the awaited reward, and it is the essence of the toil
Before we enter the explanation of their words, we must understand His thought in creating the worlds and this reality before us: His works did not come forth from Him through many thoughts, as is our way. He is One, Unique, and Unified, as above; and as He is simple, so His Lights, extending from Him, are simple and unified, without any multiplicity of forms, as it is written: "My thoughts are not your thoughts, nor your ways My ways." Therefore understand and perceive that all the names and appellations, and all the worlds, upper and lower, are all one simple Light, One, Unique, and Unified. With the Creator, the extending Light, the thought, the operation, the operator, and everything the heart can think and contemplate are with Him literally one and the same.
By this you shall judge and perceive that this entire reality, upper and lower together, to the very last at the conclusion of the end of correction, was emanated and created in one single thought. That single thought performs all; it is the essence of all the operations; it is that which receives the purpose; it is the essence of the toil; and it is itself the reality of all the completeness and of the awaited reward — as the Ramban explained, in the secret of One, Unique, and Unified, as above.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 1:9
items.html:
The matter of the restriction explains how an imperfect operation came forth from the perfect Operator
This is why the Rav [the ARI] elaborated, in the first chapters of this book, on the matter of the first restriction — for this matter is the gravest of the grave. It is inevitable that the corruptions and every kind of deficiency also extend and come from Him, as it is written, "who forms light and creates darkness"; yet the corruptions and the darkness are the true opposite of Him — how can the one extend from the other? And how do they come together with the Light and the delight in the thought of creation? We cannot say that they are two thoughts, separate from one another — God forbid to say or even to contemplate such a thing, as above. How, then, does all of this extend from Him down to this world, so full of filth, torments, and great foulness — and how are they sustained together within the single thought?
